question: how to urban farm
title: How to Urban Farm: Growing Real Food in the Middle of the City
metaDescription: Urban farming is possible on almost any scale — from a backyard in Houston to a rooftop or balcony container setup. Scotty explains how to grow living food in the city using biology-first methods that work even in small spaces.
content:
# How to Urban Farm: Growing Real Food in the Middle of the City

I'm growing vegetables in the middle of Houston, Texas. Not on some rural acreage with a barn and a tractor. In a backyard in a dense neighborhood, with neighbors on all sides, a couple of raised beds, and a whole lot of soil biology working on my behalf.

Urban farming is one of the most subversive things you can do in modern America. Not because it's illegal or dangerous — it's neither. It's subversive because every tomato you grow yourself is a tomato you didn't buy from a supply chain that picked it green, trucked it a thousand miles, and sold it to you for three times what it would have cost the farmer to produce. Every egg you collect from a backyard chicken is an egg with a yolk that's actually orange, from a bird that actually moved around and ate real food.

Here's how to do it.

## Start With Honest Assessment of Your Space

Urban farming starts with looking at what you actually have. A backyard. A balcony. A rooftop. A sunny window. A community garden plot. Each of these has different possibilities and different constraints.

For a full backyard situation in a city like Houston, you can run a serious small-scale food production operation. The USDA Climate Hubs defines urban agriculture broadly — it includes container gardening, rooftop production, community gardens, and small residential plots. All of these count. All of these can produce real food.

The primary limiting factor for most urban growing spaces is sunlight. Vegetables need a minimum of six hours of direct sun daily. Walk your space at different times of day during the season you're planning to grow. Buildings and trees cast long shadows. That shaded spot that looks bright in the morning might be completely in shadow by noon.

After sunlight, consider soil — or the lack of it. Urban soils are often heavily compacted, contaminated with heavy metals from older paint and exhaust, or covered in hardscape. If you're dealing with urban soil of unknown history, raised beds with imported growing media are safer than in-ground planting.

Grist's coverage of urban farming notes that the modern urban farming movement has innovated around the soil problem — raised beds, containers, vertical growing systems, and even hydroponic setups all allow food production without relying on potentially compromised urban ground.

## The Biology-First Approach Works Everywhere

Here's what I want y'all to understand: the biology-first approach to growing food applies at any scale. Whether you're growing in a 4x8 raised bed or a five-gallon container on a fire escape, the principles are the same.

You want living soil, not dead growing medium. You want a diverse community of bacteria and fungi working with your plant roots. You want organic matter cycling through the system. You want to feed the organisms that feed your plants rather than feeding your plants directly with synthetic inputs.

The difference between a container of potting mix from the hardware store and a container of genuinely living growing medium is massive. Potting mix is typically biologically dead — sterilized peat or coir, perlite for drainage, maybe a slow-release synthetic fertilizer pellet. It's a substrate that holds a plant up while it consumes inputs you provide.

Living growing medium — good finished compost mixed with quality topsoil, a little bit of aged wood chips for fungal habitat, active microbes from a previous growing season — is a whole ecosystem. It cycles nutrients. It protects against disease. It improves with use rather than degrading.

For urban containers and raised beds, I'd suggest building your mix from at least 30-40% finished compost, adding a handful of worm castings, and topping with an inch of wood chip mulch to protect the surface biology and retain moisture. Every season, add more compost. The biology builds over time.

## What to Grow in the City

Urban space is valuable and usually limited, so think carefully about what gives you the most return per square foot.

**Herbs** are the highest-value crop per square foot in most urban situations. Fresh basil, cilantro, parsley, chives, thyme, oregano, and mint are expensive to buy and easy to grow. A couple of herb containers can save a household $50 a month in grocery costs and deliver far better flavor than dried or even fresh-cut grocery herbs.

**Leafy greens** are the next best bang for your urban farming buck. Lettuce, kale, chard, arugula, and spinach produce quickly, can be harvested repeatedly (cut-and-come-again), and are expensive at the grocery store relative to what they cost to grow. Houston gardeners can grow these through the entire fall-winter-spring season with very little effort.

**Cherry tomatoes** are my favorite urban fruit crop. They're more productive per plant than slicers, they're more disease-resistant, they produce over a longer season, and the flavor difference between a sun-warmed cherry tomato from your garden and a grocery store tomato is enormous. One cage-supported plant in a 15-gallon container can produce pounds of fruit.

**Cucumbers, beans, and squash** are highly productive crops that work well in urban spaces if you give them vertical support. Trellising cucumbers and beans up instead of letting them sprawl on the ground multiplies your effective growing space significantly.

**Fermentable crops** are something I think about that most urban farming guides don't address. If you're growing enough cucumbers or cabbage or hot peppers to ferment, you're not just growing fresh food — you're building a food preservation practice that extends the season and produces gut-healthy living food year-round.

## Rainwater Collection and Smart Watering

Municipal water contains chlorine and chloramines. These are added specifically to kill bacteria. When you water your living soil with heavily chlorinated water, you're dosing your soil biology with a mild biocide every time you irrigate.

In Houston — and in most Southern cities — rainwater is abundant and free. A simple rain barrel attached to a downspout can collect hundreds of gallons from a single decent storm. That water is unchlorinated, slightly acidic in a way that many plants prefer, and full of dissolved organic material that feeds soil biology.

Bootstrap Farmer's urban farming guide recommends drip irrigation or soaker hoses for urban production because they put water at the root zone, reduce foliar disease from wet leaves, and use significantly less water than overhead watering. In a city where water costs money and drought restrictions are common, this efficiency matters practically as well as biologically.

## Community and Connection: The Hidden Yield

Urban farming has a social dimension that I think is actually one of its most important products. When I'm at the Urban Harvest Farmers Market here in Houston, the most valuable thing happening there isn't the tomatoes changing hands. It's the community forming.

The USDA Urban Agriculture resources note that urban farms do more than produce food — they build social capital, improve food access in underserved neighborhoods, reduce urban heat island effects through vegetation, and create educational opportunities. Grist's reporting on urban farming consistently highlights the community building function as co-equal with the food production function.

Your urban farm — even if it's three raised beds in a Houston backyard — is a node in a larger food community. It connects you to the farmers market vendors you buy from, to the neighbors you share extra produce with, to the people who notice what you're doing and ask questions. That connection is part of what makes food actually nourishing rather than just calorically adequate.

## The Honest Limitations

I'm not going to tell y'all that urban farming will replace the grocery store or that everyone in the city can feed themselves from their backyard. For most urban families, a home food production setup supplies a fraction of caloric needs. What it does supply, when done right, is the highest-quality fraction — the freshest, most biologically alive, most nutritionally dense food you'll eat all year.

SAREF's guide on sustainable urban farming is honest about this: urban farming is not primarily an economic replacement for the food system. It's a complement to it, a way of reclaiming some portion of your food supply from industrial production and putting it back in your own hands.

Modern Farmer's 2025 coverage of urban farming notes that the most creative urban farming developments are about repurposing spaces — vacant lots, rooftops, underused public land — rather than expecting everyone to grow all their food in tiny apartments. Think about what space you have and what it can realistically produce. Then build the biology and start growing.

---

- Grist. "The new wave of urban farming (and fresh food from small spaces!)." https://grist.org/article/the-new-wave-of-urban-farming-how-to-get-fresh-food-from-small-spaces/
- Bootstrap Farmer. "Urban Farming 101: What you need to know to get started." https://www.bootstrapfarmer.com/blogs/urban-farming/urban-farming-101-what-you-need-to-know-to-get-started
- SARE. "Best Practices for the Sustainable Urban Farm." https://www.sare.org/resources/best-practices-for-the-sustainable-urban-farm/
- Modern Farmer. "Repurposing Urban Spaces for Farming: 7 Creative Ideas." https://modernfarmer.com/2025/03/urban-farming-ideas/
